by me. This franchise ordinance
does not reflect my individual ideas,
and l stated to Mr.. Stranorn that |
had no doubt whatever that the or
dinance would be amended by the
city council.


Shortly after the franchise ordi-
nance was prepared, Mr.. Hamblen
took up the work for Mr. Strahorn
and l had nothing further to do with
the ordinance and never went before
the city council to urge its passage.


Am certain that Mr.. Strahorn did
not intend by the small amount of
work l did for him to in any way
embarrass me in the freight rate
mate that he expected that I would
refrain from doing anything and
everything legal or equitable to Sc
cure terminal rates. If that was his
purpose, he failed to make me under
stand it, and if he had made me so
ed our conference instantly.


In fact, at the time of my first
conference with Mr.. Strahorn, l in
formed him that ! could not and
or do anything that would in any
wise interfere with my duties or OBJ
legations in the Spokane freight rate
case or the lumber freight rate case;
that / was for terminal rates first,
last and all the time, and would do





of securing terminal rates for
Spokane.


Mr.. Strahorn. for himself person
ally at least, expressed himself as
in favor of terminal rates, and, as
| understood him, stated that his
road, being a local concern, was not
interested in terminal rates.


Further. the matter of a terminal
rate provision in the franchise was
not at that time thoroughly consid-
cred. We were then looking for re-
lief either from the interstate com
merce commission or by some legal
arrangement for the routing of
freight by one person Neither of
these has so far brought the re-
suits; so l have advised the ter
minal rate provision in the fran
chise.


The actual expense and costs have
absorbed more than one half the
fund supplied for the interstate
commerce case, consequently, my
services in connection with the
very much less remunerative than
freight rate litigation have been
you state in your article. II. NJ.
Stevens.
